title: Pubertal Gynecomastia That Is Not Physiological: Three Published Cases
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: PAIS masking as persistent gynecomastia (PMC6311465), ectopic aromatase in fibrolamellar HCC (JCEM/PMC4918295), and β-hCG-secreting mediastinal choriocarcinoma (PMC6612059) — hormone patterns, clinical pearls, and mandatory workup triggers
authors: Resident training module — three published case reports (see sourceFile)
sourceFile: Endocrinol Diabetes Metab Case Rep PMC6311465; JCEM/Endotext NBK279105 PMC4918295; Endotext PMC6612059 PMC8096621

relatedChapters.title: Gynecomastia with Markedly Elevated Estradiol (13-Year-Old Boy)

patient.chiefComplaint: Persistent or rapidly progressive pubertal gynecomastia — three distinct published cases
pmh: Case 1 (PAIS): 17M, bilateral gynecomastia Tanner 3 for 3 years, sparse body/axillary hair, no facial hair, no ambiguous genitalia history
Case 2 (FL-HCC): 17.5M, severe persistent gynecomastia >2 years, RUQ mass, hepatomegaly, normal testicular volume
Case 3 (mediastinal GCT): peripubertal male, rapid-onset tender bilateral gynecomastia, mild cough, normal testes on exam and ultrasound

caseDescription: Three published cases illustrate how pubertal gynecomastia can mask serious pathology. Case 1 — Partial Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (PAIS): 17-year-old with persistent progressive gynecomastia, high testosterone, high LH, elevated estradiol, inadequate virilization; novel AR missense mutation A721T (PMC6311465). Case 2 — Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma with ectopic CYP19A1/aromatase expression: 17.5-year-old with severe gynecomastia, estrone 1200 pg/mL, estradiol 312 pg/mL, suppressed LH/FSH, low testosterone, 1.5-kg liver tumour; hormones normalised after resection (JCEM/PMC4918295). Case 3 — Extragonadal β-hCG-secreting mediastinal choriocarcinoma: peripubertal male with rapid tender gynecomastia, high testosterone and estradiol, suppressed LH/FSH, β-hCG >5000 mIU/mL, normal testicular ultrasound, anterior mediastinal mass (PMC6612059). Module teaches when physiological pubertal gynecomastia needs workup and how to read three distinct hormonal signatures.
keyFindings: Persistent gynecomastia >2 years or progression in late adolescence mandates endocrine workup
PAIS: high testosterone + high LH + inadequate virilization = androgen resistance, not physiological gynecomastia
FL-HCC: massive estrone/estradiol + suppressed gonadotropins + low testosterone = autonomous peripheral estrogen (ectopic aromatase)
β-hCG tumour: high testosterone + high estradiol + suppressed LH/FSH + elevated β-hCG + normal testes = extragonadal GCT workup (mediastinum)
finalDiagnosis: Three distinct pathological causes of adolescent gynecomastia masquerading as pubertal gynecomastia: (1) Partial Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome — AR gene mutation with androgen resistance; (2) Fibrolamellar HCC — ectopic aromatase (CYP19A1) driven by abnormal promoter switching; (3) Primary mediastinal choriocarcinoma — tumour β-hCG stimulating Leydig cells via LH-receptor mimicry. Common lesson: duration, progression, virilization status, and the hormone pattern — not breast enlargement alone — determine the workup.
learningObjectives: Identify when pubertal gynecomastia requires pathological evaluation beyond reassurance
Recognise the PAIS pattern: high testosterone, high LH, inadequate virilization, persistent gynecomastia
Interpret autonomous peripheral estrogen production (FL-HCC) with suppressed gonadotropins and low testosterone
Apply the β-hCG extragonadal germ-cell tumour protocol when testicular ultrasound is normal
Distinguish the three hormonal signatures and select the correct imaging and genetic/tumour markers

items.title: Why persistent pubertal gynecomastia requires endocrine workup
items.content: Physiological pubertal gynecomastia is a transient state (ages ~10–16) caused by a temporary testosterone-to-estradiol imbalance — usually bilateral, often tender, and self-limiting within 1–2 years. Red flags demanding workup: persistence beyond 2 years, progression into late adolescence (as in all three cases), rapid onset with marked tenderness, unilateral predominance, failure of pubertal progression, or inadequate virilization despite apparently adequate androgens. Minimum screen: testosterone, estradiol, LH, FSH, β-hCG, liver function, careful testicular and abdominal examination. These three published cases show that gynecomastia may be the presenting — and only — sign of PAIS, ectopic aromatase-secreting liver tumour, or extragonadal germ-cell tumour.
keyPoints: Physiological: transient, <2 years, self-limiting.
>2 years or late-adolescent progression → workup.
Hormone pattern — not breast size alone — guides diagnosis.

items.title: Case 1: PAIS presenting as persistent pubertal gynecomastia
items.content: Source: Endocrinology, Diabetes & Metabolism Case Reports (PMC6311465). A 17-year-old male had bilateral gynecomastia Tanner stage 3 persisting and progressing for 3 years. Normal height and weight but sparse body and axillary hair with no facial hair. Critically, no history of ambiguous genitalia — PAIS can present in an otherwise phenotypically normal male. Labs were paradoxical for simple pubertal gynecomastia: very high total and free testosterone, elevated LH, and elevated estradiol (from testosterone aromatisation in the face of androgen resistance). Genetic testing revealed a novel missense mutation A721T in the androgen receptor (AR) gene — confirming Partial Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome.
keyPoints: 17M, gynecomastia 3 years, Tanner 3 breast.
High T + high LH + poor virilization.
AR mutation A721T — PAIS confirmed.

items.title: Why high testosterone with inadequate virilization suggests androgen resistance
items.content: In classic pubertal gynecomastia, testosterone is often normal with a relative estrogen excess — not very high testosterone with failure to virilize. When circulating testosterone is high or high-normal yet facial/body hair is sparse and gynecomastia persists, the testes are producing androgen but peripheral tissues (and pituitary feedback loops) behave as if androgen action is inadequate — LH rises attempting to compensate, and aromatisation to estradiol contributes to breast tissue growth. This pattern should trigger androgen receptor sequencing (and consideration of 5α-reductase deficiency in other contexts). PAIS can lack ambiguous genitalia at birth and declare itself only in adolescence as gynecomastia.
keyPoints: High T + high LH + poor virilization = resistance.
Not the pattern of transient pubertal gynecomastia.
AR gene testing when this pattern appears.

items.title: How PAIS can masquerade as physiological pubertal gynecomastia
items.content: The trap is reassuring an adolescent because karyotype is 46,XY, genitalia appeared normal, and testosterone is 'high.' Without examining virilization (hair pattern, muscle, voice) and duration (>2 years), PAIS is missed. Management differs: physiological gynecomastia needs observation; PAIS needs genetic counselling, endocrine follow-up, discussion of anti-estrogen therapy or mastectomy for severe gynecomastia, and fertility implications depending on PAIS grade. Always correlate testosterone with its effect, not with the lab range in isolation.
keyPoints: Normal external genitalia does not exclude PAIS.
Duration >2 years is a workup trigger.
Examine virilization, not testosterone alone.

items.title: Case 2: Fibrolamellar HCC with ectopic aromatase and severe gynecomastia
items.content: Source: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism / Endotext (PMC4918295). A 17.5-year-old boy had severe persistent gynecomastia worsening over 2 years with failure to progress through late puberty. Examination: palpable right upper quadrant mass and hepatomegaly; testes normal volume without gonadal nodules. Hormones: estrone 1200 pg/mL (normal <50), estradiol 312 pg/mL (normal <35) — massively elevated. Pituitary negative feedback: LH 2.8 IU/L, FSH 1.3 IU/L (suppressed), testosterone 1.53 ng/mL (low). CT: 1.5-kg liver tumour. Histology: fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma. RT-PCR and immunohistochemistry showed intense ectopic intracytoplasmic aromatase (CYP19A1) via abnormal promoter switching (promoters I.3 and II). Post-resection: E2 <50 pg/mL, testosterone 6.85 ng/mL, gynecomastia regressed.
keyPoints: Estrone 1200, E2 312 — autonomous estrogen.
Suppressed LH/FSH, low testosterone.
FL-HCC with ectopic CYP19A1; cured by resection.

items.title: Why fibrolamellar HCC causes feminization in young non-cirrhotic patients
items.content: Cirrhotic liver disease classically causes gynecomastia through impaired hepatic estrogen clearance and altered SHBG — but fibrolamellar HCC occurs in young patients without cirrhosis and feminizes through active ectopic aromatase production, not reduced clearance. The tumour autonomously converts androgens to estrogens at massive scale. This explains estrone disproportionately higher than estradiol and the complete suppression of LH/FSH — the pituitary sees adequate (excessive) estrogenic feedback. Any adolescent with severe gynecomastia, hepatomegaly or RUQ mass, and suppressed gonadotropins needs abdominal imaging even without liver function test abnormalities typical of cirrhosis.
keyPoints: FL-HCC: young, non-cirrhotic, active aromatase — not clearance defect.
Massive estrone/estradiol suppresses gonadotropins.
RUQ mass + gynecomastia → image the liver.

items.title: How ectopic CYP19A1 aromatase drives the hormone pattern in FL-HCC
items.content: Normal aromatase in adipose and gonadal tissue is regulated; in FL-HCC, abnormal promoter usage (promoters I.3 and II) drives intense intracytoplasmic P450 aromatase expression within tumour cells. The tumour becomes an estrogen factory — flooding circulation with estrone and estradiol, suppressing GnRH/LH/FSH, and lowering endogenous testosterone production. Breast tissue responds to estrogen excess; testes are not the source. After tumour resection, the autonomous source is removed and the axis normalises — confirming the mechanism. Lesson: when LH/FSH are suppressed with low testosterone, look for peripheral estrogen production (liver, adrenal, testicular tumour) before labelling central hypogonadism.
keyPoints: CYP19A1 ectopic expression in tumour cells.
Autonomous estrogen suppresses axis → low T.
Resection normalises hormones — proof of mechanism.

items.title: Why suppressed gonadotropins with low testosterone point to peripheral estrogen excess
items.content: A common parsing error is calling low testosterone with low LH 'hypogonadotropic hypogonadism' without measuring estradiol. In Case 2, LH 2.8 and FSH 1.3 are appropriately suppressed by massive estrogen feedback — the problem is not the pituitary failing to stimulate the testes, but an extragonadal source flooding the system with estrogen. The same principle applies whenever estradiol or estrone is markedly elevated: check for liver tumours (FL-HCC), adrenal aromatase-expressing tumours, or estrogen-secreting neoplasms. Testicular exam and ultrasound remain essential to exclude Leydig/Sertoli cell tumours.
keyPoints: Low LH/FSH + low T — measure estradiol/estrone first.
High peripheral estrogen suppresses the axis.
Not central hypogonadism until estrogen source excluded.

items.title: Case 3: Mediastinal choriocarcinoma presenting as rapid pubertal gynecomastia
items.content: Source: PubMed Central / Endotext (PMC6612059, PMC8096621). A peripubertal adolescent male presented with rapid-onset, highly tender bilateral gynecomastia — the symptom that drove presentation. Mild intermittent cough and vague chest discomfort were present but secondary. Testicular examination and dedicated testicular ultrasound: normal volume, no masses, no burned-out germ cell scars. Labs: elevated total testosterone and estradiol, completely suppressed basal LH and FSH. β-hCG profoundly elevated (>5000 mIU/mL); AFP normal. Chest CT: large anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy: extragonadal non-seminomatous germ cell tumour — primary mediastinal choriocarcinoma.
keyPoints: Rapid tender gynecomastia + cough.
β-hCG >5000, normal testicular USG.
Anterior mediastinal choriocarcinoma.

items.title: Why tumour β-hCG mimics LH and drives the hormone cascade
items.content: β-hCG shares an identical alpha subunit with LH, FSH, and TSH; its beta subunit has strong structural homology with LH. Tumour-derived β-hCG directly stimulates Leydig cell LH receptors → massive testicular testosterone production. Testosterone is aromatised to estradiol (often out of proportion), causing gynecomastia. High testosterone and estrogen exert negative feedback → endogenous LH and FSH are suppressed. Thus the pattern is high testosterone, high estradiol, low LH/FSH, and very high β-hCG — with a normal testis on ultrasound because the tumour is extragonadal. This is LH-receptor mimicry, not primary testicular pathology.
keyPoints: β-hCG activates LH receptor on Leydig cells.
↑T → aromatisation → ↑E2 → gynecomastia.
Feedback suppresses endogenous LH/FSH.

items.title: How to work up extragonadal germ cell tumour when testicular ultrasound is normal
items.content: Protocol: any peripubertal/adolescent male with rapid gynecomastia, elevated β-hCG, and normal testicular ultrasound requires immediate chest CT (anterior mediastinum — commonest extragonadal site in males) and consideration of retroperitoneal and pineal imaging as indicated. Measure AFP and LDH for tumour typing (choriocarcinoma: very high β-hCG, AFP often normal). Do not stop at 'pubertal gynecomastia' when β-hCG is elevated. Extragonadal germ cell tumours are chemosensitive but time-critical — delay from misdiagnosis as benign puberty is dangerous.
keyPoints: β-hCG elevated + normal testes → chest CT mandatory.
Mediastinum commonest extragonadal site in males.
AFP, LDH, biopsy for tumour classification.

items.title: How to distinguish the three hormonal signatures at a glance
items.content: PAIS (Case 1): HIGH testosterone, HIGH LH, elevated estradiol, inadequate virilization — androgen resistance with compensatory LH rise. FL-HCC (Case 2): VERY HIGH estrone/estradiol, SUPPRESSED LH/FSH, LOW testosterone — autonomous peripheral estrogen. Mediastinal GCT (Case 3): HIGH testosterone, HIGH estradiol, SUPPRESSED LH/FSH, VERY HIGH β-hCG, normal testes — LH-receptor mimicry from extragonadal tumour. Physiological pubertal gynecomastia: mild imbalance, normal virilization progressing, self-limiting <2 years, no tumour markers elevated. The workup branches on PTH-independent-style logic applied to gynecomastia: measure testosterone, estradiol, LH, FSH, β-hCG, examine and ultrasound testes, examine abdomen and chest.
keyPoints: PAIS: high T, high LH, poor virilization.
FL-HCC: massive E2/E1, low LH/FSH, low T.
GCT: high β-hCG, high T/E2, suppressed LH/FSH.

items.title: Consolidated clinical pearls from three published cases
items.content: Pearl 1: Persistent gynecomastia >2 years or late-adolescent progression requires endocrine workup — not reassurance alone. Pearl 2: High testosterone with poor virilization and high LH → androgen resistance (PAIS) even without ambiguous genitalia. Pearl 3: FL-HCC feminizes young non-cirrhotic patients via ectopic aromatase — not the cirrhosis clearance mechanism. Pearl 4: Suppressed gonadotropins with low testosterone → measure estradiol/estrone and image liver/adrenals/testes. Pearl 5: Elevated β-hCG with normal testicular ultrasound → chest CT for mediastinal germ cell tumour. Pearl 6: Rapid-onset tender gynecomastia in a male is a tumour red flag until β-hCG and imaging exclude it.
keyPoints: Duration and progression trigger workup.
Three distinct hormone signatures.
β-hCG and imaging are non-negotiable in atypical cases.

items.question: Why should very high testosterone with sparse body hair and persistent gynecomastia raise suspicion for PAIS rather than physiological pubertal gynecomastia?
items.answer: Because physiological pubertal gynecomastia occurs with normal pubertal progression and typically resolves within 2 years — virilization continues (facial/body hair, voice, muscle). Very high circulating testosterone with inadequate virilization and elevated LH indicates the tissues are resistant to androgen action — the pituitary raises LH to compensate while aromatisation produces estrogen driving gynecomastia. This is androgen resistance (PAIS), not a transient estrogen-testosterone imbalance. AR gene testing is indicated.

items.question: How does fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma produce severe gynecomastia in an adolescent without cirrhosis?
items.answer: Tumour cells express ectopic aromatase (CYP19A1) through abnormal promoter switching, converting androgens to massive quantities of estrone and estradiol. This autonomous peripheral estrogen suppresses LH and FSH via negative feedback and lowers endogenous testosterone. The adolescent presents with severe gynecomastia, hepatomegaly, and a suppressed hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis — not because of impaired hepatic estrogen clearance (the cirrhosis mechanism) but because of active estrogen production by the tumour. Surgical resection removes the source and normalises hormones.

items.question: Why are LH and FSH suppressed in mediastinal choriocarcinoma despite elevated testosterone?
items.answer: Tumour-derived β-hCG stimulates Leydig cells through the LH receptor, driving high testicular testosterone production. Testosterone and its aromatised product estradiol exert negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary, suppressing endogenous LH and FSH. The testosterone is secondary to tumour hCG action, not primary testicular autonomy — and the testes appear normal on ultrasound because the neoplasm is extragonadal. The suppressed gonadotropins therefore reflect feedback from high sex steroids, not central hypogonadism.

items.question: How should an adolescent male with persistent gynecomastia >2 years be worked up to catch these three pathologies?
items.answer: Take duration, rate of change, virilization status, and tenderness. Labs: testosterone, estradiol (and estrone if estradiol very high), LH, FSH, β-hCG, AFP, LDH, liver function tests. Examine testes and abdomen; testicular ultrasound; if β-hCG elevated with normal testes, chest CT for mediastinal mass. If estradiol/estrone massively elevated with suppressed gonadotropins, abdominal CT for liver and adrenal tumours. If high testosterone with high LH and poor virilization, AR gene sequencing for PAIS. Do not attribute >2-year or rapidly progressive gynecomastia to physiology without this panel.

items.stem: A 17-year-old male has gynecomastia for 3 years, very high testosterone, elevated LH, sparse facial hair, and no ambiguous genitalia history. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: High testosterone with inadequate virilization and elevated LH indicates androgen resistance — PAIS (AR mutation A721T in published case). Physiological gynecomastia resolves <2 years. Klinefelter has low testosterone and high FSH.

items.stem: A 17.5-year-old boy has estrone 1200 pg/mL, estradiol 312 pg/mL, LH 2.8 IU/L, FSH 1.3 IU/L, testosterone 1.53 ng/mL, and a liver mass. What is the mechanism?
options: Impaired hepatic estrogen clearance from cirrhosis
items.explanation: FL-HCC with ectopic aromatase drives massive autonomous estrogen, suppressing gonadotropins. This occurs in young non-cirrhotic patients — not the cirrhotic clearance mechanism.

items.stem: An adolescent male has rapid tender gynecomastia, testosterone and estradiol elevated, LH/FSH suppressed, β-hCG >5000 mIU/mL, and normal testicular ultrasound. What is the next essential imaging step?
options: Chest CT for anterior mediastinal mass
items.explanation: Elevated β-hCG with normal testes mandates search for extragonadal germ cell tumour — anterior mediastinum is the commonest site in males. Published case: mediastinal choriocarcinoma.

items.stem: Which feature most strongly indicates pathological rather than physiological pubertal gynecomastia?
options: Persistence beyond 2 years with progression in late adolescence
items.explanation: All three published cases had persistence >2 years or progressive severe gynecomastia into late adolescence — a mandatory workup trigger per clinical pearls.

items.stem: Which hormone pattern is characteristic of tumour β-hCG-mediated gynecomastia with extragonadal germ cell tumour?
options: Low testosterone, high LH, low β-hCG
High testosterone, high estradiol, suppressed LH/FSH, very high β-hCG
Low testosterone, low LH, normal β-hCG
items.explanation: β-hCG stimulates Leydig cells → high T and aromatised E2; feedback suppresses LH/FSH. Normal testicular USG points to extragonadal source.

items.stem: After surgical resection of fibrolamellar HCC in Case 2, what happened to hormone levels?
options: Estradiol remained elevated; testosterone fell further
Estradiol normalised (<50 pg/mL) and testosterone rose to 6.85 ng/mL; gynecomastia regressed
LH rose but gynecomastia persisted unchanged
items.explanation: Removing the ectopic aromatase source normalised estrogen, relieved feedback suppression, and restored testosterone — gynecomastia regressed (published case outcome).

items.stem: What genetic finding confirmed PAIS in Case 1?
items.explanation: PMC6311465: novel missense mutation A721T in the androgen receptor gene confirmed Partial Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome.

items.stem: A boy has high testosterone, HIGH LH, and poor virilization. Which diagnosis fits best?
items.explanation: High LH with high testosterone indicates compensatory drive against androgen resistance — PAIS. FL-HCC and GCT show suppressed LH/FSH; physiological gynecomastia has normal virilization progression.

items.statement: PAIS always presents at birth with ambiguous genitalia.
items.explanation: False. Case 1 had no ambiguous genitalia history — PAIS can present in adolescence as persistent gynecomastia with inadequate virilization.

items.statement: Fibrolamellar HCC causes gynecomastia primarily through impaired estrogen clearance in cirrhotic liver.
items.explanation: False. FL-HCC occurs in young non-cirrhotic patients and feminizes via active ectopic aromatase production (CYP19A1), not clearance failure.

items.statement: β-hCG can stimulate Leydig cells because its structure shares homology with LH.
items.explanation: True. β-hCG activates the LH receptor on Leydig cells, driving testosterone and (via aromatisation) estradiol production.

items.statement: Physiological pubertal gynecomastia typically resolves within 1–2 years.
items.explanation: True. Persistence beyond 2 years or progression in late adolescence — as in all three cases — requires pathological evaluation.

items.statement: A normal testicular ultrasound excludes the need for chest imaging when β-hCG is profoundly elevated.
items.explanation: False. Normal testes with high β-hCG mandate extragonadal germ cell tumour search — chest CT for mediastinal mass is essential.

items.statement: In FL-HCC-associated gynecomastia, LH and FSH are typically suppressed by estrogen feedback.
items.explanation: True. Massive estrone/estradiol from ectopic aromatase suppresses gonadotropins — Case 2: LH 2.8, FSH 1.3.

items.assertion: Chest CT should be performed when β-hCG is elevated and testicular ultrasound is normal in an adolescent with gynecomastia.
items.reason: Extragonadal germ cell tumours, especially mediastinal choriocarcinoma, secrete β-hCG without a testicular mass and are a life-threatening cause of rapid gynecomastia.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Published Case 3 had mediastinal choriocarcinoma with normal testicular imaging.

items.assertion: Androgen receptor gene testing is indicated in an adolescent with high testosterone, high LH, and inadequate virilization.
items.reason: This hormone pattern indicates androgen resistance rather than androgen deficiency, and PAIS is confirmed by pathogenic AR mutations.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Case 1 was confirmed by AR missense mutation A721T.
